hero.h1: Mercedes-Benz G-Class W463 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the second-generation G-Class W463 — from the 3.0-litre straight-six diesel to the 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 monsters. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the M177 that defines the AMG legend. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [PATTERN DATA].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes-Benz G-Class W463 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the second-generation W463 specifically — from the entry-level G 350 d diesel through to the range-topping AMG G 63.

overview.intro: The second-generation Mercedes-Benz G-Class W463, introduced in 2018, represents the most comprehensive overhaul in the G-Wagen's history. While retaining its iconic boxy silhouette and body-on-frame construction, the W463 brought a completely revised chassis, a new front axle, improved aerodynamics, and a significantly upgraded interior. The engine range was equally transformed — the naturally aspirated and older turbocharged V8s gave way to modern twin-turbo units: the M176 4.0-litre V8 in the G 500 (422 bhp), the AMG-tuned M177 in the G 63 (585 bhp), and the all-new OM656 3.0-litre straight-six diesel in the G 350 d (249 bhp) and G 400 d (330 bhp). The 500,000th G-Class unit — a special one-off model — was built in April 2023, marking the model's enduring success.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes-Benz W463 (second generation, 2018–2024) • Predecessor: W463 (first generation, 1990–2018) • Successor: W465 (2024–present) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (five-door SUV only).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W463 G-Class attracts strong specialist interest in the UK used market, with M176 V8 and OM656 diesel enquiries dominating pre-purchase inspections and high-mileage maintenance planning [PATTERN DATA].

items.quote: Thin-Data Fallback mode applied — compressed to a single paragraph. Best & Worst Engines: The W463's engine range is tightly focused but every unit is exceptional. At the top for everyday usability sits the OM656 3.0-litre straight-six diesel — the G 400 d (330 bhp) offers effortless torque (700 Nm), strong economy and proven durability, making it the most sensible G-Class for daily driving. The G 350 d (249 bhp) is a more affordable entry point but still delivers the same robust OM656 architecture. Among petrols, the M176 4.0-litre V8 in the G 500 (422 bhp) is smooth, refined and benefits from cylinder deactivation for improved efficiency — it's the pick for buyers who want V8 character without AMG running costs. The M177 in the AMG G 63 (585 bhp) is a masterpiece — brutal, addictive and genuinely quick for a 2.5-tonne SUV — but it demands deeper pockets for fuel, tyres and specialist maintenance. For most buyers, the G 400 d represents the sweet spot between capability, economy and running costs. Who it's for: buyers who want the sweet spot between G-Class presence and running-cost sanity.

economicsRule.title: The W463 rule
economicsRule.text: A £6,000–£10,000 M176 recon against a £40,000–£80,000 W463 sits at just 7.5–25% of the car's value — well within Mercedes-Benz's 70% threshold. Even a £10,000–£14,000 M177 recon on a £60,000–£100,000 AMG G 63 sits at 10–23%. The G-Class's extraordinary retained values mean engine replacement is almost always financially viable. For OM656 diesels, the economics are even more favourable — a £5,000 recon on a £45,000 example is just 11%. Value the car, but know that the G-Class holds its value like almost nothing else on the road.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W463 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: G 350 d (OM656) — excellent fuel economy, lower entry price.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W463 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: AMG G 63 (M177) — extreme performance, extreme running costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W463 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: G 400 d — the sweet spot between performance and running costs.

problems.title: OM656 AdBlue System & EGR Cooler Issues
problems.rootCause: The OM656's AdBlue system (SCR) can suffer from crystallisation in the injector and NOx sensor failures. EGR cooler leaks are also documented, particularly on higher-mileage examples. These are common modern diesel issues rather than design flaws specific to the OM656, but they can be costly to rectify if left unaddressed.
problems.recommendation: Have the AdBlue system and EGR cooler inspected at every major service — a £600–£1,000 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£4,000 replacement that follows a full system failure.

cta.label: Compare W463 OM656 AdBlue repair quotes →

problems.title: M176/M177 Coolant Leaks (Thermostat Housing & Turbo Lines)
problems.rootCause: The hot-vee design of the M176 and M177 concentrates heat between the cylinder banks, accelerating gasket and seal degradation on the thermostat housing and turbocharger coolant lines. Coolant loss can lead to overheating if not addressed promptly.
problems.recommendation: Monitor coolant levels regularly on any M176 or M177-engined G-Class — a £800–£1,400 thermostat housing replacement is cheap insurance against a £3,500+ engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare W463 M176/M177 coolant system repair quotes →

problems.title: M177 Turbocharger & Injector Wear (AMG G 63)
problems.rootCause: The M177 produces 585 bhp from 4.0 litres — an extreme specific output that places significant stress on the turbochargers, injectors and cooling system. Cars that are driven hard or tracked can experience premature turbo wear and injector failure.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying an AMG G 63, full service history is essential — a £1,200–£2,200 preventative turbo inspection is far cheaper than the £8,000+ replacement that follows a failure.

cta.label: Compare W463 M177 turbo repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: W463 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-Benz G-Class W463 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned W463 engine typically costs between £4,000 and £14,000 supply only [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet], depending on engine — the OM656 diesel sits at £4,000–£7,000, the M176 V8 in the G 500 at £6,000–£10,000, and the M177 in the AMG G 63 at £8,000–£14,000. Fitting adds 12–22 hours of labour at specialist rates — budget an additional £780–£1,430 for installation. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

items.question: Which Mercedes-Benz G-Class W463 engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The OM656 3.0-litre straight-six diesel (G 350 d/G 400 d) is widely regarded as the most durable W463 engine — Mercedes' modern straight-six diesel architecture, proven in S-Class and GLE applications, offering strong fuel economy and excellent longevity. Among petrols, the M176 4.0-litre V8 (G 500) is robust and refined.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz G-Class W463?
items.answer: AdBlue system and EGR cooler issues on the OM656 diesel, coolant leaks from the thermostat housing and turbo lines on the M176/M177 V8s, and turbocharger/injector wear on the AMG G 63's M177 are the most documented issues. Each requires different preventative strategies.

items.question: Is the Mercedes-Benz G-Class W463 reliable?
items.answer: Yes — the W463 represents a significant engineering upgrade over its predecessor, with modern engine architectures across the range. The OM656 diesel and M176 V8 are generally reliable with routine maintenance. The M177 in the AMG G 63 is a high-performance engine that demands more frequent servicing and specialist attention, but it's well-proven in the wider AMG lineup.

items.question: Which Mercedes-Benz G-Class W463 engine should I avoid?
items.answer: No engine outright — all W463 engines are robust and well-engineered. However, the AMG G 63 (M177) is a specialist proposition with significantly higher running costs — fuel, tyres, servicing and insurance are all in a different league. The G 350 d offers the lowest entry cost but the G 400 d delivers noticeably more performance for a modest premium.

items.question: How do I know if my W463 engine needs replacing?
items.answer: AdBlue warning lights or poor running (OM656 diesel), coolant loss (M176/M177 V8s), turbocharger whine or loss of power (M177), or blue smoke from the exhaust are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a W463 engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: The G-Class's extraordinary retained values mean engine replacement is almost always financially viable. A £6,000–£10,000 M176 recon on a £40,000–£80,000 G 500 is just 7.5–25% of the car's value — well within our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. Even a £10,000–£14,000 M177 recon on a £60,000–£100,000 AMG G 63 is just 10–23%. Replacement is almost always justified.

items.question: How long do Mercedes-Benz G-Class W463 engines last?
items.answer: The OM656 diesel can comfortably exceed 150,000 miles with proper servicing. The M176 V8 is similarly durable, though coolant system maintenance is key. The M177 in the AMG G 63 is robust for its output but will demand more frequent maintenance, particularly on cars that are driven hard.

items.question: What is the difference between the W463 and W465?
items.answer: The W463 (2018–2024) is the second-generation G-Class, introducing a revised chassis, new front axle, modern V8 and straight-six engines, and a significantly upgraded interior. The W465 (2024–present) is the third-generation G-Class, with electrified powertrains and further refinement. This page covers the W463 specifically. The W463 is also sometimes referred to as W463A in some markets.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz G-Class W463 engine code?
items.answer: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on OM656/M176/M177 units. Tell us your variant and approximate year and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.
